title: The Open Cluster Chemical Abundances and Mapping Survey: IX. Measuring the Effects of Stellar Diffusion in the Open Clusters NGC 752 and Ruprecht 147 using APOGEE
comment: Accepted for publication in the ApJ
summary: A growing understanding of stellar processes that alter surface chemical abundances over time has opened new avenues for using these changes as probes of stellar properties. On the main sequence and near the turnoff, stellar surface abundances are affected by gravitational settling and radiative acceleration, collectively known as atomic diffusion. In this work, we use SDSS/APOGEE DR17/DR19 data to investigate atomic diffusion in the open clusters NGC~752 and Ruprecht~147, thereby constraining how these signatures vary with age. From the analysis of Fe, C, N, Na, Mg, Al, Si, S, K, Ca, Ti, V, Cr, Mn, Co, and Ni, we find significant abundance differences between stars near the turnoff and the cooler main-sequence, where warmer stars are depleted relative to the cooler main-sequence stars at the $\geq1σ$ level for all elements available in the analysis. These abundance differences are consistent with the signatures expected from atomic diffusion and are further supported by comparisons with stellar models that include diffusion. By fitting the observed $T_{\rm eff}$--[Fe/H] patterns with MIST isochrones, we obtain best-fit ages of 1.0~Gyr for NGC~752 and 2.5--3.2~Gyr for Ruprecht~147. Carbon is a key diagnostic, as it shows both atomic diffusion and extra-mixing signatures associated with first-dredge-up. For NGC~752, the coolest stars in our sample, which provide the best proxy for the initial cluster composition, yield [Fe/H]$_{\rm CS} = 0.01~\pm~0.02(\pm~0.05)$ dex. For Ruprecht~147, we obtain [Fe/H]$_{\rm CS} = 0.17~\pm~0.00(\pm~0.05)$ dex. Our findings further constrain atomic diffusion models, suggesting that atomic diffusion affects age estimates of stars near the main-sequence turnoff.

AI.tldr: This study uses SDSS/APOGEE data to investigate atomic diffusion in open clusters NGC 752 and Ruprecht 147, finding systematic abundance differences between turnoff and cooler main-sequence stars consistent with diffusion models, and deriving cluster ages of ~1.0 Gyr and 2.5–3.2 Gyr respectively.
AI.motivation: To understand how atomic diffusion (gravitational settling and radiative acceleration) alters surface abundances over time on the main sequence, and to constrain how these diffusion signatures vary with age using open clusters as testbeds.
AI.method: Analyzed Fe, C, N, Na, Mg, Al, Si, S, K, Ca, Ti, V, Cr, Mn, Co, and Ni abundances from SDSS/APOGEE DR17/DR19 for NGC 752 and Ruprecht 147; compared observed Teff–[Fe/H] patterns with MIST isochrones including diffusion; used coolest stars as proxies for initial cluster composition.
AI.result: Significant abundance differences (≥1σ) found between warm turnoff stars (depleted) and cooler main-sequence stars for all 16 elements; best-fit ages of 1.0 Gyr (NGC 752) and 2.5–3.2 Gyr (Ruprecht 147); initial [Fe/H] of 0.01±0.02 dex (NGC 752) and 0.17±0.00 dex (Ruprecht 147); carbon shows both diffusion and extra-mixing (first-dredge-up) signatures.
AI.conclusion: Atomic diffusion signatures are confirmed and age-dependent; diffusion affects age estimates for stars near the main-sequence turnoff, and must be accounted for in stellar population studies.

title: Preferential alignment of Class 0, Class I protostellar disks in multiple systems across nine nearby molecular clouds
comment: 39 pages, 25 figures, 4 tables. Accepted by A&A 2026.7.15
summary: Protostellar disk orientations in multiple systems provide critical insights into the primary mechanisms that govern the formation of multiple-star systems, their subsequent dynamical evolution, and their impact on planet-forming disks. We present a disk alignment study of 512 Class 0, Class I, and flat-spectrum protostars across nine nearby molecular clouds within 500 pc, utilizing data from the CAMPOS and VANDAM surveys. Our sample includes 74 binaries and 31 high-order multiple systems. We find that multiple systems with projected pair separations up to 6000 au exhibit preferential disk alignment with respect to each other across all evolutionary classes, deviating significantly from the random distribution predicted by turbulent fragmentation models. This suggests that the formation of multiple systems cannot be explained by turbulent fragmentation alone. Disk alignment on scales of a few thousand au is also difficult to explain by disk fragmentation as the dominant origin. We further find that the degree of nearest-neighbor disk alignment in higher-order multiples is comparable to that in binaries. Finally, we identify a significant deficit of flat-spectrum protostellar disks in high-order multiple systems as compared to younger Class 0 and Class I phases. The decline is consistent with rapid dynamical evolution, in which most higher-order systems dissolve by the end of the Class I phase.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 512 protostars (including 74 binaries and 31 high-order multiples) across nine molecular clouds reveals that multiple systems with separations up to 6000 au exhibit preferential disk alignment across all evolutionary classes, contradicting random alignment expected from turbulent fragmentation. Higher-order multiples show comparable alignment to binaries, and a significant deficit of flat-spectrum disks in high-order systems suggests rapid dynamical dissolution by the end of the Class I phase.
AI.motivation: To determine the dominant formation mechanisms of multiple-star systems by analyzing protostellar disk orientations in young multiple systems, thereby distinguishing between turbulent fragmentation, disk fragmentation, and dynamical evolution scenarios.
AI.method: A disk alignment study of 512 Class 0, Class I, and flat-spectrum protostars from the CAMPOS and VANDAM surveys across nine nearby molecular clouds within 500 pc, focusing on 74 binaries and 31 high-order multiple systems with projected separations up to 6000 au.
AI.result: Multiple systems show non-random preferential disk alignment up to 6000 au across all evolutionary classes; higher-order multiples exhibit alignment comparable to binaries; flat-spectrum protostellar disks are significantly deficient in high-order multiples compared to younger classes.
AI.conclusion: Turbulent fragmentation alone cannot explain the observed alignment patterns, and disk fragmentation is also unlikely as the dominant origin on scales of thousands of au. The deficit of flat-spectrum disks in high-order systems suggests rapid dynamical dissolution of most higher-order systems by the end of the Class I phase.

title: Positive superhumps in high mass ratio cataclysmic variables driven by apsidal disk precession
summary: Previously it has been assumed that a cataclysmic variable (CV) disk can only become eccentric and display superhumps if the 3:1 resonance is located within the disk. This requires the binary mass ratio to be $q=M_2/M_1\lesssim 0.33$, where $M_1$ is the mass of the white dwarf and $M_2$ is the mass of the companion star. However, several systems with higher mass ratios have been observed to exhibit positive superhumps, posing a challenge to this picture. We present the first 3D hydrodynamic simulations to show that eccentricity growth can occur in CV disks even when the resonance radius lies outside the disk. The finite width of the 3:1 resonance extends into the outer parts of the disk and drives eccentricity. While high mass ratio CVs more commonly show negative superhumps, our linear analysis reveals that the direction of apsidal precession is highly sensitive to the disk outer radius and surface density distribution. Smoothed particle hydrodynamic simulations tend to suppress eccentricity gradients and favor prograde precession. These results provide a natural explanation for positive superhumps in high mass ratio CVs and show that disk structure, rather than the resonance location alone, controls the emergence of superhumps.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the assumption that CV disks require the 3:1 resonance to be within the disk for eccentricity and superhump formation. Using 3D hydrodynamic simulations, the authors show that even when the resonance lies outside the disk, its finite width can still drive eccentricity in the disk's outer regions. They also demonstrate that apsidal precession direction is sensitive to disk structure, explaining positive superhumps in high mass ratio CVs.
AI.motivation: Observations of positive superhumps in cataclysmic variables with mass ratios q > 0.33 contradict the established theory that the 3:1 resonance must lie within the disk, motivating a re-examination of eccentricity growth mechanisms in CV disks.
AI.method: 3D hydrodynamic simulations and linear analysis were used to study eccentricity growth and precession in CV disks, examining the role of the finite width of the 3:1 resonance even when its radius lies outside the disk.
AI.result: Eccentricity can grow in CV disks even when the 3:1 resonance radius lies outside the disk, due to the resonance's finite width extending into the disk's outer regions. The direction of apsidal precession is highly sensitive to the disk outer radius and surface density distribution.
AI.conclusion: Disk structure, not just resonance location, controls superhump emergence, providing a natural explanation for positive superhumps in high mass ratio CVs.

title: Re-evaluating solar irradiance reconstructions: No evidence for large secular trends
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary: Most current irradiance reconstructions suggest only a modest secular increase in TSI between the 1700 and 1986 activity minima ($<1$ Wm$^{-2}$). However, 2 models, the CHRONOS and Penza et al. (2024; PEA24) models, suggest substantially larger changes of 2.1-5.9 Wm$^{-2}$. Although the two models differ in their architecture, both use cosmogenic isotope records combined with neutron monitor data to describe the long-term irradiance variability. However, cross-calibrating modulation potential reconstructions from cosmogenic isotopes against neutron monitor data remains highly uncertain. We reassess the origin and the magnitude of the large secular trends in the CHRONOS and PEA24 models. We update the CHRONOS and PEA24 models using recent heliospheric modulation potential and open solar flux reconstructions based on geomagnetic data and neutron monitor measurements, which provide a more reliable connection between cosmogenic isotope and neutron monitor records. These allow the secular component of the reconstructions to be extended consistently to the satellite era and compare the resulting TSI reconstructions with direct TSI measurements. We find that the original CHRONOS and PEA24 reconstructions substantially overestimated the secular variability in irradiance. When constrained by direct TSI measurements, CHRONOS yields a TSI increase of about 0.1-0.44 Wm$^{-2}$ between the 1700 and 1986 minima, while PEA24 returns about 0.2-0.25 Wm$^{-2}$. Our analysis indicates that the previously inferred large secular trends arose primarily from improper linking cosmogenic isotope and neutron monitor records, together with issues in the adopted smoothing approach. The updated reconstructions presented here point toward a relatively modest secular increase in TSI since the Maunder Minimum, likely below 1 Wm$^{-2}$, consistent with the majority of current irradiance models.

AI.tldr: This paper reassesses two TSI reconstruction models (CHRONOS and PEA24) that previously suggested large secular increases (2.1-5.9 Wm⁻²) since the Maunder Minimum. By updating them with geomagnetic and neutron monitor data, the authors show the original models overestimated secular variability. Corrected reconstructions yield modest increases of 0.1-0.44 Wm⁻² (CHRONOS) and 0.2-0.25 Wm⁻² (PEA24), consistent with most current models below 1 Wm⁻².
AI.motivation: To resolve discrepancies between conventional irradiance models showing modest secular TSI increase (<1 Wm⁻²) and two outlier models (CHRONOS, PEA24) suggesting substantially larger changes (2.1-5.9 Wm⁻²), by identifying and correcting methodological errors in cross-calibrating cosmogenic isotope and neutron monitor records.
AI.method: Updated CHRONOS and PEA24 models using recent heliospheric modulation potential and open solar flux reconstructions based on geomagnetic data and neutron monitor measurements. Extended the secular component consistently to the satellite era and compared resulting TSI reconstructions against direct TSI measurements.
AI.result: Original CHRONOS and PEA24 models substantially overestimated secular variability. Updated CHRONOS gives 0.1-0.44 Wm⁻² increase between 1700 and 1986 minima; updated PEA24 gives 0.2-0.25 Wm⁻². Large secular trends arose from improper linking of cosmogenic isotope and neutron monitor records and issues in smoothing.
AI.conclusion: The updated reconstructions point to a relatively modest secular TSI increase since the Maunder Minimum (likely <1 Wm⁻²), consistent with the majority of current irradiance models, and attribute the previously large estimates to methodological flaws in cross-calibration.

title: Runaway OB stars within 1 kpc of the Sun
summary: Runaway stars are high-velocity stars ejected from their birth environments that can provide insights into the kinematic history of the stellar cluster they were ejected from. We derived runaway star probabilities for 40 O-type stars and $24,487$ B-type stars taken from a recently published volume-complete sample of OB stars within 1 kpc of the Sun. We fit a Galactic rotation model to the observed proper motions of these stars and identify runaway stars using both a fixed 2D peculiar velocity threshold of $23\,km\,s^{-1}$ and by comparing individual peculiar velocities to the dispersion of the whole sample. We find runaway fractions of $17.5^{+0.1}_{-2.5}\%$ for O-type stars and $6.9\pm0.1\%$ for B-type stars; both using the fixed velocity threshold method. These values are consistent with previous studies, but with differences that are largely attributable to the underlying samples of OB stars used in various studies to identify runaway stars and to variations in the methods used to select them.

AI.tldr: This study derives runaway probabilities for 40 O-type and 24,487 B-type stars within 1 kpc of the Sun, using a Galactic rotation model and both fixed (23 km/s) and dispersion-based peculiar velocity thresholds. Runaway fractions are 17.5% for O-type and 6.9% for B-type stars, consistent with prior work, with differences attributed to sample composition and selection methods.
AI.motivation: Runaway stars provide insights into the kinematic history of their birth clusters; understanding their fractions among different stellar types helps reveal the dynamical evolution and ejection mechanisms in stellar clusters.
AI.method: A Galactic rotation model was fitted to proper motions of OB stars within 1 kpc. Runaway stars were identified via a fixed 2D peculiar velocity threshold of 23 km/s and by comparing individual peculiar velocities to the sample dispersion.
AI.result: Runaway fractions: 17.5% for O-type stars and 6.9% for B-type stars using the fixed velocity threshold. Results align with previous studies, with discrepancies explained by sample differences and selection methods.
AI.conclusion: The derived runaway fractions are consistent with prior work; differences stem from variations in the underlying OB star samples and the criteria used to identify runaway stars.

title: On the CME productivity of solar active region 13664/8
comment: 14 pages, 13 figures, accepted for publication in Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: In May 2024, NOAA active region (AR) complex 13664/8 was one of the most productive regions of the current solar cycle, producing 12 X-class flares and over 20 coronal mass ejections (CMEs) and triggering the strongest geomagnetic storm since 2003. We investigate why this AR complex was so CME-productive. Using primarily SDO/AIA and SDO/HMI observations, we analyzed its photospheric magnetic evolution, eruption sources, eruption waiting times, and magnetic parameters in comparison with five other ARs. The region initially contained only AR 13664 and exhibited limited flare activity until AR 13668 emerged on May 4, after which clustered major flares and CMEs occurred. Rapid, complex flux emergence substantially increased the region's area, magnetic flux, complexity, and nonpotentiality. The increased complexity was manifested by at least 12 emerging bipoles and 6 collisional polarity inversion lines (cPILs) formed between nonconjugated polarities, all showing sustained collision and shearing and serving as eruption sources. Decay index distributions show systematically lower critical heights (<45 Mm) above CME source regions. The CME waiting time distribution exhibits two peaks, suggesting that multiple cPILs enhanced CME productivity by increasing both recurrent CMEs from the same source and disturbance-triggered CMEs from nearby sources. These results indicate that, in addition to sufficient nonpotentiality and rapid background-field decay, high magnetic complexity accompanied by dynamical collisional shearing at multiple cPILs was crucial for the extreme CME productivity of AR 13664/8.

AI.tldr: NOAA AR 13664/8 was an extremely productive active region in May 2024, producing 12 X-class flares and >20 CMEs. Using SDO/AIA and SDO/HMI data, the study finds that rapid, complex flux emergence after AR 13668 emerged on May 4 created multiple collisional polarity inversion lines (cPILs) and sustained shearing, low decay index critical heights, and two-peak CME waiting times. High magnetic complexity with dynamical collisional shearing at multiple cPILs drove extreme CME productivity.
AI.motivation: To understand why NOAA AR complex 13664/8 was exceptionally CME-productive, producing the strongest geomagnetic storm since 2003.
AI.method: Analyzed photospheric magnetic evolution, eruption sources, waiting times, and magnetic parameters using SDO/AIA and SDO/HMI observations, comparing with five other ARs.
AI.result: Rapid complex flux emergence increased area, flux, complexity, and nonpotentiality. 12 emerging bipoles and 6 collisional PILs formed; decay index <45 Mm above sources; CME waiting time distribution showed two peaks indicating recurrent and disturbance-triggered CMEs.
AI.conclusion: Extreme CME productivity requires not only sufficient nonpotentiality and rapid background-field decay, but also high magnetic complexity with dynamical collisional shearing at multiple cPILs.

title: Efficiency of Tidal Dissipation in Convective Flow Under Rapid Tidal Forcing
summary: For close binaries and star-planet systems, tidal interactions mediate the energy transfer between the orbital motion and the internal flows of the bodies involved, thus playing a central role in their evolution. For equilibrium tides, the associated energy transfer is commonly modeled through an effective viscosity acting on the tidal flow. However, the scaling of viscous dissipation efficiency with tidal frequency $ω_\text{T}$ remains debated, particularly when $ω_\text{T}$ greatly exceeds the convective eddy turnover frequency $ω_\text{c}$. Previous numerical studies have addressed this issue by subjecting a turbulent convective flow to an oscillating background shear mimicking equilibrium tides. In this work, we adopt a novel three-layered convective box -- designed to represent a stellar convection zone sandwiched between two stable layers -- driven by an external periodic forcing. We quantify tidal dissipation efficiency by the forcing power on the flow in steady state. Our results yield a shallower scaling of tidal power per unit mass with $ω_\text{T}$ than reported in earlier shear-flow simulations. This scaling is consistent with the prediction by \cite{Terquem2021}, suggesting that the effective turbulent viscosity depends only weakly on $ω_\text{T}$, although our simulations are restricted to $ω_\text{T}\lesssim 10ω_\text{c}$. Moreover, we find no evidence of inverse energy transfer (or ``negative viscosity''), a phenomenon observed in some prior shear-flow simulations. We further investigate the influence of rotation within the same local framework. Slow rotation ($Ω\lesssim ω_\text{T}$) tends to enhance the tidal power, whereas fast rotation ($Ω\gtrsimω_\text{T}$) significantly suppresses it. We discuss the limitations of our approach and the broader implications of our findings.

AI.tldr: This paper studies tidal dissipation in convective zones using a three-layered convective box model with external periodic forcing. The authors find a shallower frequency scaling of tidal power than previous shear-flow simulations, consistent with Terquem (2021), and no evidence of negative viscosity. Rotation enhances tidal power at slow rates but suppresses it at fast rates.
AI.motivation: The scaling of viscous dissipation efficiency with tidal frequency (especially when ω_T ≫ ω_c) remains debated, and previous numerical studies using oscillating background shear have yielded conflicting results.
AI.method: A three-layered convective box (representing a stellar convection zone sandwiched between two stable layers) is driven by external periodic forcing to mimic equilibrium tides. Tidal dissipation efficiency is quantified by the forcing power on the flow in steady state.
AI.result: (1) A shallower scaling of tidal power per unit mass with ω_T than earlier shear-flow simulations, consistent with Terquem (2021), suggesting weak dependence of effective turbulent viscosity on ω_T for ω_T ≲ 10ω_c. (2) No evidence of inverse energy transfer (negative viscosity). (3) Slow rotation (Ω ≲ ω_T) enhances tidal power; fast rotation (Ω ≳ ω_T) suppresses it.
AI.conclusion: The findings support a weak frequency-dependence of turbulent viscosity for equilibrium tides, caution against negative viscosity claims from prior studies, and reveal a non-monotonic rotational dependence of tidal dissipation.

title: Search for double-line spectroscopic binaries in RAVE survey
comment: link to datatable is provided in text
summary: I present a study for double-line spectroscopic binaries content in spectra from the RAVE spectra, using composite spectral model. Three complementing selections found 2813 composite spectra, belonging to 2605 unique targets. Also these results were compared with automatic classification based on t-SNE map, which shows good agreement. Additionally I identified several spectra affected by problems with wavelength calibration.

AI.tldr: A study identifying double-line spectroscopic binaries in RAVE spectra using composite spectral modeling, finding 2813 composite spectra (2605 unique targets), with results validated against t-SNE automatic classification and identification of wavelength calibration issues.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize double-line spectroscopic binaries within the RAVE spectral dataset using composite spectral modeling, and to validate these findings against automatic classification methods.
AI.method: Used composite spectral models to analyze RAVE spectra; employed three complementary selection methods to identify composite spectra; compared results with t-SNE-based automatic classification.
AI.result: Identified 2813 composite spectra belonging to 2605 unique targets; found good agreement between the composite model selection and t-SNE automatic classification; also detected several spectra with wavelength calibration problems.
AI.conclusion: Composite spectral modeling is effective for detecting double-line spectroscopic binaries in RAVE data, and the results are consistent with unsupervised classification methods, while wavelength calibration issues were noted in some spectra.

title: Stellar Multiplicity in Open Clusters: Investigating Binary Fractions and Their Relationship with Cluster Properties
summary: Stellar multiplicity is key in dynamical evolution of open clusters (OCs) and processes of stellar formation and evolution. Since most stars form in multiple systems, investigating how environmental properties affect their occurrence is crucial. We aim to investigate how binary fraction and mass ratios change across different OC environments. Using a sample of 773 OCs, we identified binary systems and estimated binary fractions, obtaining values in the ranges 0.10-0.85 overall and 0.06-0.79 for high mass-ratio systems. No strong dependence on age or evolutionary stage is found, while an anticorrelation between metallicity and the binary fraction is identified. However, young and old clusters exhibit distinct mass-ratio distributions. We also find that the probability of a star being the primary component increases with stellar mass. In contrast, the probability of a star belonging to a binary system increases with mass for masses below 1 solar mass, and remains between 60% and 70% for high masses. Binary systems are preferentially located in the inner regions of clusters compared to single stars, and the mean mass ratio decreases with increasing distance from the cluster center. These results suggest that the binary fraction appears to be set at early times and remains largely unaffected by cluster dynamical evolution. The concentration of binaries in the inner regions of clusters likely reduces their susceptibility to dynamical escape.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes 773 open clusters to investigate how binary fraction and mass ratios vary across different cluster environments, finding that binary fraction (0.10-0.85 overall) shows no strong dependence on age but anti-correlates with metallicity, with binaries preferentially located in inner cluster regions and the probability of being in a binary system increasing with mass below 1 solar mass.
AI.motivation: To investigate how stellar multiplicity (binary fraction and mass ratios) changes across different open cluster environments, given that most stars form in multiple systems and environmental properties may affect their occurrence.
AI.method: Analyzed a sample of 773 open clusters, identified binary systems, and estimated binary fractions (overall: 0.10-0.85; high mass-ratio: 0.06-0.79), examining dependencies on age, metallicity, stellar mass, and spatial distribution within clusters.
AI.result: No strong age/evolutionary stage dependence; anticorrelation between metallicity and binary fraction; distinct mass-ratio distributions between young and old clusters; probability of being primary increases with mass; probability of being in binary increases with mass below 1 solar mass, stays 60-70% for high masses; binaries concentrated in inner cluster regions; mean mass ratio decreases with distance from center.
AI.conclusion: Binary fraction is set at early times and largely unaffected by cluster dynamical evolution; binary concentration in inner regions likely reduces their susceptibility to dynamical escape.

title: The non-LTE abundances of magnesium and yttrium and asteroseismic ages for the chemical clock calibration
summary: Building on our previous study, which demonstrated the importance of accounting for departures from local thermodynamic equilibrium (LTE) in elemental-abundance determinations and of using asteroseismic ages, we investigate spatial variations in the empirical [Y/Mg]-age relation across the Galactic disc using a substantially larger stellar sample. We analysed high-resolution stellar spectra and determined Mg and Y abundances through spectral synthesis of multiple spectral features, rigorously accounting for non-LTE (NLTE) effects. We derived asteroseismic ages for stars exhibiting solar-type oscillations and used cross-checked isochrone-based methods for the remaining stars. We determined atmospheric parameters and Mg and Y abundances for 528 Galactic field stars, together with asteroseismic ages for 307 stars and isochronal ages for 221 stars. We also identified two new triple-lined and nine double-lined spectroscopic systems. Combining the present sample with that of our previous study yielded a total of 736 stars, which we used to examine the [Y/Mg]-age relation across the Galactic disc. The relation shows systematic spatial variations that likely reflect differences in star-formation and chemical-enrichment histories. In general, [Y/Mg] tends to increase with metallicity over the investigated age range. At supersolar metallicity, however, this trend may weaken, and the [Y/Mg]-age relations become flatter than those of solar-metallicity stars, which show lower [Y/Mg] values at young ages and higher values at old ages.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes 528 Galactic field stars (736 total with prior data) to investigate spatial variations in the [Y/Mg]-age relation across the Galactic disc, finding systematic variations likely due to different star-formation and chemical-enrichment histories, with [Y/Mg] generally increasing with metallicity but showing flatter relations at supersolar metallicity.
AI.motivation: To investigate spatial variations in the empirical [Y/Mg]-age relation across the Galactic disc using a substantially larger stellar sample, building on previous work that highlighted the importance of accounting for NLTE effects and using asteroseismic ages.
AI.method: High-resolution stellar spectra analysis with Mg and Y abundances determined via spectral synthesis of multiple features, rigorously accounting for NLTE effects. Asteroseismic ages for 307 solar-type oscillating stars; isochrone-based ages for 221 remaining stars. Atmospheric parameters determined for 528 Galactic field stars, with identification of 2 new triple-lined and 9 double-lined spectroscopic systems.
AI.result: Combined sample of 736 stars (528 new + 208 previous) reveals systematic spatial variations in the [Y/Mg]-age relation across the Galactic disc. [Y/Mg] generally increases with metallicity. At supersolar metallicity, the relation flattens compared to solar-metallicity stars, which show lower [Y/Mg] at young ages and higher at old ages.
AI.conclusion: Spatial variations in the [Y/Mg]-age relation reflect differences in star-formation and chemical-enrichment histories across the Galactic disc. Accounting for NLTE effects and using reliable age determination methods (asteroseismic + isochronal) are crucial for robust abundance-age relations.
